speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: This is Axel Anderson, interview number five.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, you heard it that before, you know, that there's no change in that one. We got all the locomotives together. We had three of them, potlats had three of them, and Milwaukee was there with one or two, and we had the sawmill there and got them all together. I went down and talked to the night watchers, and we got them all together, and they pulled the locomotives all together and tied down the whistle string and let it go on for about five minutes. Long stay of steam. Yeah, that's quite a record there at 12 o 'clock, well, at midnight.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I think everybody was up anyway. There was a nice, friendly little town when Alcarver, when the mill was running, you know, it was just like a big family.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We lived in the same house there for 20 years, and we didn't own a key. We never locked the door.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Some people, sometimes they come in, or friends come in, and they might have been at a card party or something, you know, and come on in at three or four o 'clock in the morning, woke us up, talked a while and started from home.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, that is a pretty nice little place.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do they have dances and stuff there? Huh? Do they have dances?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yes. Oh, you bet. They had a big gymnasium for the school, you know, they could use, and they had an outfeller hall, and they had a masonic hall. It was big enough to dance. Gymnasium was plenty big, but they could get up 10, 15 couple at a time.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they had a balcony on there, you know, get out on the floor and get up on the balcony as I can clean around it. That was good. I think that burnt up.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell us a story about the time you took the payroll?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was in Minnesota.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I thought that was, I've been thinking, I never thought anything about it then. That was the first winter I was here in the country, in the winter of 19, 5 and 6. You know, I was thinking afterwards, you know, funny, because I was a big kid, but leaving me and sending me out to pack that payroll, that was in money. You had to pay envelopes, you know, I had an old, I don't know what I had to carry it in, or I had an old weed sack or something on my back. I don't know. But I walked along this roadbed, you know, and from one cabin or shack to another one, those people were batching, you know, camped along the roadbed there in the swamps.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And there was a lot of money. There was a heck of a lot of money in there, and they were thirsting me with that to send me out and pack it. I went from one end of it to the other. I think I stayed overnight in one cabin. But you know, to send a kid out with that, I thought that afterwards I was wondering why in the heck they would do that. Not pick on some reliable old man to carry that out.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What kind of money was being paid out in that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, that was paper money, and the East was paper money.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you visit large amounts to some of these guys?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, some of them had, some of them envelopes had up to a thousand dollars or more.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know how much. I had no idea. I know there was money in it. There was money in them envelopes and over each man.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But it was funny that to me it was afterwards dead, and I didn't think nothing about it, because it was just in a day's work. I was paid by the day. I think I had a dollar and eighty cents a day, I think, at that time.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did most of those guys just take their pay and split? Did most of the guys just take their pay and leave with it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh no, some of them didn't. They stayed down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was just that one in particular I told you about. When I come back, he was already and he walked back into the railroad. And I just happened to see him when he came back. Back from Duluth, where he had to blow in. He was only going three or four days, I guess, and he come back and boy, I tell you, he blew it. And he was blue.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he had the same overall and the same old jacket. He didn't get any clothes that he was going in to get. He forgot all the orders.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was he depressed at all that he'd done that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, I don't know whether he was or not. I guess he didn't feel too bad. He didn't feel very good anyway. Either mentally or physically. First day, they gave him a good going over. I tell you, his face was just like an old hamburger cut up. Blue and the other was all cuts.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know if he even had a haircut. I can't remember now, but it hung in my mind for quite a while.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would most of the lumberjacks out here, in the early years, do the same thing? Would they take their payrolls?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no. No payrolls. No, they were paid in camp, you see. The other companies said I worked for them, I didn't work for any. I meant to ask where the lumberjacks would take their pay.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: They'd go into town and blow it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yes. Sure they did. Sure they did. Them years, you know. 40 years ago. 30 years, too. Yes, you better take them into town and blow it. Just in a few days. Never took a sober breath from the time they come in till they left. Maybe some saloon keeper, where they spend the most to know about them, take it and put them on the train and send them back.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Give them a bottle and send them back. Get another one. Get another steak.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'm still trying to figure that out, Axel. Because from what you say and what other guys say, it seems like just about all lumberjacks blew in. Well, nowadays, everybody tries to save a little money. It's always save, save. It sounds like then nobody's saved, hardly.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is there, do you know why it was that way? Everybody spent everything?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I couldn't tell you. I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The lumberjack, you know, there was a lot of married men working in the crews. But they had study jobs, you know, and that was at floating population. There were so many camps. It was so much different from what it is now. There is no comparison. You know, each and every one of us, you know, when I first come out here, you carried your own blanket. And in Minnesota, where I worked in Wisconsin, when I worked in the camps there, you know, they had, the lumber companies had camps, you know, and they had blankets in there. And I don't know if they ever was laundryed from the time they put them in the camp till the camp burnt up or burnt down or something. They were lousy and bed bugs and whatever, everything that you could think of was in them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And when I come back west, you know, each one of us had to carry our own blanket.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And you know what that happened?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You go from one camp to another, and whatever got into them blankets, you carried from one place to the next one. Up until the strike they were doing, World War I, when they had to change it. That took away the blanket bundle. The company finished blankets after that. And clean sheets, pillow slips, and... Oh, there was no comparison in it. And the lumberjack, you know, he was regularly an outcast, you know. There was nothing for him to look forward to or anything. So I guess whenever he had a little fun, he thought he had it when he got into town and got drunk.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now, ladies, you know, they haven't... I don't know if there is any more camps in the woods or not. There's too many roads down, everybody got cars, and the chains, you know, the lumberjack, he lives in town. He lives among other people. And there isn't any lumberjack like it used to be.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now it's... You know, they got to know they had to run most of them. They got to get their dogs now as cat drivers, and they're the mechanics, and they're the best of the land compared to what it used to be.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Some of them go out there in the camp, you know, and they stay for... Oh, sometimes they stay up to a year. A lot of them did make it a point that they want to stay a year. We had an old bullcook there where I was there one time, and they asked him this. His name was Nils Munson. He died there in Spokane. Shortly after he thought he had saved up enough so he could live the rest of it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: They asked him one time, he said, how long Nils, how long have you been in the camp?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said, let me see, he said, I've been here two years and 17 months and so many days. That he hadn't been to town. He hadn't been to town, you see, in that length of time. Two years and 17 months, he said.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That was putting there three years and a half, wasn't it?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were they sort of hermits or something?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no, they were good. Oh, heck no. They were generous. They were wide open. By God, they were generous. Anybody come over there, they won't help the others, and you bet. They didn't take nothing, the money too much, you know. If you need the money, sure, you got some. Oh, no, they were very...

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How come the guys wouldn't go to town just for an afternoon if they were close to town?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You couldn't make it. It would take you days to get to town. See, there were no trucks and no cars. Well, some of the camps were pretty near. On the railroad, but you had to take the train. If you want to go anyplace, you had to take the train. It wasn't like that now, you know. Now you could be up there in the Idaho woods and hop in the car in two hours in Spokane. Then, you know, you had to walk out and get down to the...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I meant that they didn't even go to Elk River, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, yes. Oh, they did. They did. But, you know, there was no facilities to stay very much, you know. They had boarding houses and places and a restaurant too. Oh, yes. They got down there all night.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the Jacks older men in the old days?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, they were mostly older. Older ones.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I never... they were younger ones too. I was a kid when I started in. Yes, started young, I think.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were a lot of the Lumberjacks, you know, had they worked in the woods back east before they came out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Some of them had. Some of them had. And most of them was from there. From around there. Here, you know, Spokane. But they drift, you know. They had to go. I know one man that come up in 1911 and he started out hiking from what he took to the area. They would always let them ride on the train, you know. They could get in the boxcar or something, you know, because the train stopped at every station and get off where they wanted and there were a lot of camps. But he took, he said, he was telling me, he was six weeks going from St. Mary's to Elk River. He never lost a meal and he always had a bed every night. And he never had a cent.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He didn't always put them up in the camp, you know. Jack come in there, he stayed overnight, he had supper and breakfast. If he, sometime they were full and he couldn't get on and other times they asked him and he didn't like it so he took his bundle or pack sack and started for the next camp. Stayed overnight there. This particular man, you know, he said he put in six weeks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And he never done a day's work and he never lost a meal and he always had a bed. You couldn't do that nowadays, there isn't anything like that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You haven't told us anything about, much about gambling. And I have had the idea that that was a good kind of recreation in those days. No, no, all they did was play kibbles and pinoc. No, they never seen any gambling in the camp. They didn't allow it because they didn't, if they started gambling, you know, that meant a fight. And you always had trouble over it. So there was no gambling. That was an unwritten law I think right in the camp. No gambling.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But that meant trouble, whenever they started to gamble over money that meant trouble.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And you very, very seldom see a fight in the camp. For some reason everybody got along. We worked together there for months and when I got too much, somebody pulled out. Go to the next camp.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, when you read about the old long days some places, they make a big deal out of that. Fights, you know, they say there's a lot of fights for lumberjacks. Fought all the time. I've never really believed that. Well, honestly, I never saw it and I was in camp, around the camp there for 40 years.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I didn't work on the phone outfit so I didn't go. Only if we went to some other place, we just went there to see how they were logging, what method they were using and see if we could improve anything, if we see anything that would help us.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I never did see any fights in the camp.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is there anything that some of the lumberjacks would do to pass the time, like sing songs or read?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, you bet they read everything that was lost. Anything they could find, you bet.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You mean they'd read anything they could find?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, if there was any reading method there, but you didn't have any magazine like we have now, you know. The only thing would be books, you know, somebody bring in newspaper once in a while. Of course, later years, you know, we had the newspaper come up to camp every day in the last few years. But in the beginning there wasn't too much of that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When I first came out there to hide the one up in the timber there, we didn't. Well, like I told you in Elk River, we got in there, I walked in there in the first few days of, I don't know if the last days of October or the first few days of November, I don't remember. In 1909, walked in from Bowell. There was a rail up to Bowell from Pfluss. But there was, the Milwaukee wasn't, they were building it. That road wasn't in there yet. And no rails into Elk River, sort of. There, we got snowed in there in December. And I walked out for the mail in April. That's the first mail, you see. From November to April when I walked out into Bowell and got snowshoes and got it. So there wasn't much news. No telephone.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We didn't get the telephone in until then. Oh, in 10, when the rail come in, because then we had the phone. And then they brought in a phone over, they brought in another line from Morfino. First line we had, you know, was the railroad.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I guess the company could talk over that. Got in there at Bowell and talk over that line for a few months, a few days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But there was no telephone office there. I think they were the clear water. Some out there in Orphino, Snyder, I think, they brought the phone in there first. And put it in an office in Elk River.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So you call out and then to call, you had the ring, they ringed the phone them days, you know. And I think they called, they could call Orphino and if you want to get to Spokane, they go to Orphino and...

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Colfax, I think. And they need to Spokane. One station, one office rang the next one, however they were connected, you know. And it would take sometimes five minutes, many minutes, you know, before you could get to the, if you could get them at all.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: System wasn't very good.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Dick Farrell?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, you bet you. Yes, bet you. He preached the last, sometime, first there I liked him very good. But I think he got a little greedy towards the end. But the best sermon I ever heard him speak was at Avery, Idaho.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: On a... They call him the lumberjack evangelist. He go from one camp to another, you know, he had a pack sack and he... He could... He had a pass, you know, he could ride on the trains wherever he wanted to go, so he never paid anything. And wherever he come to the camp, you know, there was always a bed for him and he got his meals. Stook up a little collection. He'd get in the dining room, you know, dining car, whatever it was, give a little sermon in the evening and next day he walked to the next camp.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But he gave a good one there at Avery, and I talked to him, I said, oh, I knew him well, and I told him that's the best one I ever heard you give.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were his sermons very long?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No. No, not very long. He'd tell a story and he'd give a little prayer and he'd read a little... He'd read a little chapter of the Bible. And he'd tell a story and he'd read a little chapter of the Bible. No, he mostly just talked and tell about what he saw in the other camps and how they were doing it over there and who he met and he saw so and so.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Only thing I didn't like about it when we start to use the fist on the table or something, by God, I didn't like that. That one shouldn't go with a prayer, no need to pound it in. When he was a young fellow, he was a price fighter, what he was telling me. He's a husky guy. I wouldn't want to tackle with him.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would any other guys come out on the bridge?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, only the Beeson. No, I didn't hear of any.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When he pounded his fist there, was that the fire and brimstone part of it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know what that meant.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Some preachers tell you you've had it and you're going the other place. Oh, I've seen some of them on the pulpit, you know, pick on the devil or hit with the fist or something. Tell you what's going to happen to you and about how big a sinner he was and everything. I guess they made it more impressive, probably thought they'd give it to you a little stronger. I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It never appealed to me very much.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were the men pretty respectful of him?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, nobody ever said anything, no. Oh no.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would some guys leave and not stick around anymore?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, oh yes, the sister of one and then walk out, oh yes.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, some of them liked him and others didn't, that's natural.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I heard that there were peddlers that used to come to the camps. Oh you bet, you bet. You bet they had peddlers come there, selling stuff, watches and clothes. You had underwear and shirts and... Now we had one, I think he's living down there yet. I think he was an old lumberjack. He had, he was peddling watches, good ones too, and rings and so forth, you know. He'd do pretty good business there when he was in camp, always. He'd always sell a few watches and a few rings and a few watch chains and so forth, you know. You bet. You bet.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, there were several peddlers, they let them come in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't think they ever paid anything for staying there. I don't think anybody ever cared. There were so many meals given away so that that was in the business. Nobody was turned down.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would some guys sort of hang around, you know?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, well then, oh yes, some of them, then they were putting it up to the camp for them to go and tell them, You're a visitor over, you better get your pack so I can go. You've been there long enough. Better change the boat now, get another one.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Go from camp to camp, you know, and they work a little, work a day or two, you know, and then go another one. That way they had a few dollars and they always had a place to go. They had a bunk and, oh yes, there were a lot of them too. Didn't stay any place, not in one place, always a little green around the other side of the fence so they went over and kept going.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, we met a lot of them there, you know, and I don't think there was a camp in the, the States that they hadn't visited or seen.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes. Albert mentioned a story yesterday about one time when this guy got stuck up over, above Clarkie someplace and the first storm, Bad winter storm snowed his truck in, I guess he was up after some scrap or something the way he remembered it, and you sent up a bulldozer to, or some big machinery to get him out.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, that could be, I don't know, that was a many thing happened, no, there were a lot of things that I couldn't, never thought anything more about. Oh, that could happen.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Which happened every so often, so that didn't, but that was something, you know, if I don't ever pay any attention to it, if you give them a little lift, okay, that was alright.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of, did that happen, did men get lost or snowed in?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Not very often, no. The Jacks had pretty good, they had pretty good directions, you know, they could find their way in the timber. I was lost a few times myself, you know, especially in the snowstorm, if it was snowing, when you haven't got the sun. The sun was your best help, then you know which way you want to head or go to try to get back. I got lost a few times that way, for a few, not too long, but I got out on the wrong places, you know, when you get out in the timber and it's winter and summer, it changed, you know. It's always changing. So I got out where I find myself again and I was a few miles farther away from where I wanted to go and I was in the beginning.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And waiting in snow up to midsection.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And especially if it got dusk or dark on, you know, that's worse. When you can't see the sun, your helper is gone.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You don't know whether you're going east, west, south or north. All you can see is up through the timber and see the sky and then you're lost. But I never got lost so I had to stay overnight and then I always made it out.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you bother to take a compass with you ever?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no. I never carried a compass. No, I never carried a compass.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, you could always find the section lines. In 1911, they surveyed that part of Idaho. It hadn't been surveyed up to that time.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So then they put in section corners and quarter posts and blazed the line, you know, they could always follow it. And where we were working, you know, we blazed up the line a little more itself, you know, cut a little back off the trees, you know, so you could see where the line was. So they always find a section corner and you know where you are. They were marked, you know. They used to put in a big post there about that high and on there, timber scribe and put in section on four side. Maybe section one and two, three and four, you know, and you know which way you want to go and which and you know where you are. So we had no trouble.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The cruisers always, he used it because he had to. The only time I ever used it was when I was with the cruisers. I done pretty well that one time I, we were out in the woods and we had to pace out four 40s. You had to go so many paces and they were in L shape and I paced around them and all when I got to the fourth corner that would be a mile and something. I was only 10 paces off from the corner after I got around them. That was pretty good. I figured I'd take about three foot to the step. I had to count the paces, I had to count the steps as I went. And then when I got so far, you know, then turn right angle or left angle, you know, go so many tallies again, then turn again and go another direction.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You didn't have a compass for that?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, I had a compass. I had a compass. I followed that, you know, looked ahead as far as I could see, you know, take a mark and walk to that and then set up again. But I had to keep my, in my mind, you know, how far I went. So I had to know exactly where I was. And I was only just a few paces off when we got around.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Even the cruiser thought I was pretty good because I hadn't done a equal out of that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He counted the trees and I tell him where we was. And he had a book, you know, he marked down. He marked down as we went and they counted the trees. That's how they bought the timber then. They call that the estimate. He was estimating the timber, you see, how much that would be on an acre.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would he measure the trees or just guess?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, just guess on them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Take a neighbor, just in his own mind, you know. He can tell pretty close where they would be. See a tree like that, you know, that...

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That wouldn't be a, that wouldn't probably be a 300 feet of lumber, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Because I'm little trees, they didn't mow, they didn't bother with, you know, just anything. They were solids.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And then, then when he went along, you know, he'd mark whether there was a white pine, white fir, red fir, cedar. In spaces and then the trees, you see, he counted them to so, so much distance. And then when he come home, you know, and then he had it all together. And he goes so much and then he'd double up, you know. What he didn't see, he figured that was all about the same.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Maybe they get pretty close to the... The company would buy that timber on, on his estimate, see. Sometime cut over, sometime he cut under. That was a guess.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much idea about how much he could cruise in a day?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, I don't know, I don't remember. We were at it for... But we did when we all went to there, I know I couldn't tell you that. Overlocated roads and at the same time...

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Who was that you worked with?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, his name was Bill Helmer. Oh, that's an old cruiser. He was one of the first cruisers for Potlet. I think he come out from the East. Bill Helmer and... End of Rome.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Bill there, he was the manager. I think they come out with him. Before Potlet was started there. I think, I believe they come out there in 1901, they were telling me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And cruised, then the Potlet, then the company buy up timber. Buy up something about the land and all private and state and government. They bought the timber and not the land. And had so many years to log it. And the mill wasn't built yet.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So first day when they started they had to hustle and get to them passions of timber, you know, to log them off, get the timber off them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Or else they go back to the state.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What year was that that you, you know, did that cruising? Oh, that I did?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, that was in the late, let me see, I guess that was in the 20s.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The early 20s, I think.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you make it back to camp every night?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, we had, we found a cabin, you know, where there'd be some stone and timber. You know, you could take a stone and timber claim and do a little bit of improvement and you get a patent on it from the government. Well, there were a lot of them cabins there and you could always find a place to stay. They were gone, but the cabin was there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now we stayed about half way between El Cribble and Orphino. It wasn't too busy in that. That man there, his name was Neitzer. He was clerking in a store there in El Cribble later.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And we found his cabin. There was a heck of a nice cabin. He built it, set the post up and down, all of them. By golly, there was a nice cabin. And he had all the paper inside with newspaper stuck on the wall all around it. Now we must have stayed there with a good roof on it. We put in weeks there in that old cabin, then we found another one. Moved, worked from this one as far as we could, you know, go back and forth, evening, morning and evening, snowshoeing. We were on snowshoes all winter.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: So, those old timber claims, those stone and timber claims were sold to Potlatch, Potlatch Bottom? Is that what happened?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Sometimes Potlatch Bottom and some other fellas. And some of them go back to the state, never knew. If they didn't keep on paying taxes, no, but they finally go back to the state. Or something, somebody bought it, they go in and sell it to the company. Home status and timber claims.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I never had sense enough to get in on that.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you see many wild animals when you were out there in the woods?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You bet, you bet. Oh yes, they were all around you. Elks, deers, bears and coyotes. Once in a while you could get a glimpse of a wild cat. Not very often, they were shy, they stayed away, and so were the coyotes, they didn't get very close. But the deers, you know, they stop and look at you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: First most cow I ever saw up there, that was off from Avery up at Windy Peak. And there I look out and I get scared the hell out of me, I tell you, that was the most ugliest thing that I ever seen. I come walking down the trail, you know, and the huckleberry bushes, you know, going over the trail was putting there, filled up. And I come on down, I swear I saw that damn face looking at me, and I tell you, I didn't know what to do. So I stood still and finally she turned and boy I didn't move. I got out of there in a hurry. But dear ugly, I mean they look at you there, they don't look good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: One day I was in Avery, I was in the west, we got up there logging. That time I was looking, just looking for a campground.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And before we got started up in there, see where we could build a campground, it would be pretty well in the center, I was going to get the road in. But later after we got the road in and I had a pickup driving up along the road and the other side moves in front of me. She run for a ways, and I finally she lay down right in the middle of the road. And I get up, I get put in there up to her, you know, she faced me and her eyes was just red, just like too red. They were just shiny. She tackled the car. So I'd wait and by God I said to her, I think I was on the road there for two hours that night, I didn't get up to camp till after midnight.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So I told her, Duly was my assistant, and I told her what happened that night. I said I was down there putting that two o 'clock before I got up there. She says, you wouldn't do that to me. Okay, but I said I had a lot to respect for her, I waited. I wouldn't go and attack her. She said I'd take the ax and go after her. I said maybe you would, but I wouldn't. I waited, I took my time. Oh, there wasn't a few days later.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You know, I went out there and I looked at this pickup and I said what happened to the hood?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said that goddamn moose got on it. He thought he could scare, make her afraid, you know, she turned around and she just gave that top right in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I was wishing if I'd had a gun I'd have shot that damn thing, but I didn't have anything. I had an ax alright, but I wasn't going to take it with the ax.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The cow was worse than the bull. They were lead run, you know.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever go up and see a shed yangle in Bogle?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I've talked to him on the phone, but I haven't visited him.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I plan to, to see him.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: He's about the only one there in Bogle now, isn't he?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Him and Oloni Smith. Oh yes, you've got a couple of them car lines there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The Vanderbilt, they're all gone I guess. I don't know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But the old timers that I knew, they're practically all out of there.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: When you were out, you know, in the woods and, you know, or around camp you saw, you know, a deer or an elf, would you try to shoot it to have them meet for camp or anything?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no, no.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I shot one deer one time and put it in a mulligan. The cook did. Nobody ever knew it, they didn't know. Nobody ever mentioned it. Cook and, well the cook was a bunch of me included. We had a few steaks out of it, the rest of it they throw it in that stew pot.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, we never, never shot any meat for the camp, no. They did in Minnesota. I bought the section house there one time. We had deer meat three times a day all winter. Section boss there, he'd take a couple of his men and send them out there in the swamp and they come home in two or three days.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Had a little bit of saw bellies, you know, a little bacon, not much. Mostly deer meat all winter.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Was there much bear hanging around the camps?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was up at the everywhere, we had a dump counter, we put the garbage over in the side hill and we just throwed out the garbage not too far from the camp. You could see it, you could count twenty of them or more, less. They were there all day, digging. The kids were pretty well cleaned up. But finally they got too close, so they started to come into camp. Then they had to move that. Then the garbage wagon went out and would go about a mile away and dump it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And when that garbage truck come, you know, by God they'd be sitting all around there waiting for them just as soon as they had a hand load of dibs, took over.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They didn't try to fight us any. They still had to save this.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Wife was with me there one summer and we had a cabin there and the bear come up there by God and put the screen door on and she had the door open. The bear come there and she had a shotgun and she popped them right in the belly. But that little bird shot, but he didn't tear the cabin down. He took off. I think they, I believe they shot that bear there in the fall and when they skinned him, that damn little bird shot, you know, they just got through the skin and that's all. They fell out. When they were skinning him, then little pebbles were falling all over the floor.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They had that shotgun of mine up there at the kitchen and the flunkies were out there popping out the bear all the time.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We had the ladies flunkies there one time when I come walking down the camp and I saw two bear cubs heading for the camp and I watched them and they got under the dining car. So I went in the kitchen and I told them, the cook and the falcon said, no, I got in there and all of them was right in the middle of the floor. When they scrubbed the floor, you know, they had a hole in there so they let the water run through. And there were all three, the whole bunch of them laying around there and all looking.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I said, what the hell you got?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was going to tell them about those bear cubs. They were feeding them. They had them underneath. They knew what they had done until bear cubs knew where they were going. Them flunkies had been feeding them, you know, probably there for weeks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they were looking at them, you know, because you could see the buildings was on skids, you know, and up to daylight under the light so they could see them. And they were giving them cookies and stuff, you know, and they were at that M2 item and it was underneath there, you know. That's why they come. They knew it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The only time they got mad at them was when they dumped the garbage barrels they had there, you know. Some morning when they come up, you know, they were all scattered all over the kitchen. The platform was there back of the kitchen. So then the coke could take. So then we had to move and get the garbage way out. And we had to tie them barrels to the wall so they couldn't tip them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And they get up there in the night after everything's quiet and then the bear come around and they tipped them barrels over and picked what they wanted and the rest was left.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do they have any women flunkies in the beginning when you were in the woods?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Not in the beginning, no. But after the World War, war, you see, during that time they started in the camps. See, all the young fellas were gone and the old fella wouldn't, they were too slow to be around in the kitchen, you know, only cooking. But for flunkies, you know, they had to be light on their foot to travel. And the young fellas, you know, they were all out in the war and in the army so they started to get the women in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Were some cooks in the camps, I mean I've heard that some cooks in some, you know, form and didn't want to have women flunkies in, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, yes, there was a lot of that too.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But were there, why didn't they want them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know, see it,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: when I was, I was with the better, he's alive now too, he lives in Lewiston. We worked together in the CC camp up there on the CNCO and one night, you know, he lived in Bovill. We went home one time, both of us, and they had trouble in the camp, but we were gone so, so I told him, I said, after this we never go together, you and I. He was my assistant, you see. So I said, well now, you take, if you want to go to Mary's, alright, you go down and I'll stay. Next Sunday if I want to go, well I said, then you stay. So after that we did. And he come home there one Sunday evening, he was very lit up and he was singing. He said if there was no women's, well they have no war. And I said, maybe that was the idea in the camp too, you know, whereas the women more or less connected, you know, it's more or less trouble or jealousy or something. You don't, and in the kitchen crew, you know, they show favors and so forth, you know, and I guess that made little difficulty, more difficulty for the cook, I think. That's why they had it, they didn't like him, you know, in the camp. Otherwise I couldn't see him, you know, for my part it was alright.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I could see the cook's idea, alright. You know, it could if we make a little jealousy in the camp.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I've heard that a lot of the cooks were touchy, they were sort of a touchy bunch about their cooking and they'd get pretty sensitive. Oh yes, yes, I bet.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You mean if someone complained?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, I don't know.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: They were mad or something?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, they were just a pretty touchy bunch. Well, you know, you take, you know, even at home, you can start right from home. You know, you eat at home all the time and you get out and buy a meal and it isn't any better, but boy how you think that tasted good.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And boy how you eat, isn't that right?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You eat your own home cook until you get tired of it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Don't you feel that way?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I get tired of cooking. Yes, yes, and you get tired of your own cooking.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: In other words, don't you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, that's the same in the camp, you know, you sit with the same cook, you know, he'd be there and he'd cook every day for the whole year. You know, he can't change it very much. Where you have 150 up to 200 people to cook for, you know, you can't change your menu too much. And it's got to be the same thing over and over and over again. Every morning you have bacon, eggs and hot cake. And next morning you have ham and eggs and hot cake. Next day, you know, you go back to the ham and eggs again or the bacon and eggs and sometimes you have sausage.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That's all I like, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And a bowl of marsh. And for dinner, you know, well that's the same, a big roast or a big mulligan or stew or something. You know,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: you can't change it. It's big, you know, you got to have so much of it so you can't change it. You got to stay right there. And you get tired of it. The crew, you know, they say that old mulligan mixer, it's no good and it never was any good and it is good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Blame the cook, you know, they won't take off or take a vacation and stay away a few days, you know, that somebody come in and, well his ideas were a little bit different and it was a little different. It probably wasn't half as good as what you had before and not enough of it. And so forth and they say, boy now, that is a good cook. That is a good one. That tasted good. That was fine. A few days, you know, and then by golly, then it was different. But for a few days, you know, everything tasted good. It was a little different taste, you know, they had a little different way of seasoning or something. Oh, I tell you, a cook, that was quite a job. Albert was good. He was dandy. I never heard any. I don't think I ever heard anybody kick on him.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He said that the men talked a lot about the chuck in the camps, I think. Oh, you bet they. Well, that was the main thing, was the chuck, what they had in the camp, you know, that what held them. If they were feeling good, you know, by God, they could have stayed. I bet.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, if you didn't have a good cookhouse, boy, then you were up. You were in an eggable place.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: You know, that's quite a job. They're like we had over 300 there. No, there's 350 men, and Albert stayed there all the way through.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That old mentor, he had a helper, he had an assistant, second cook, and a baker.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That's all? Is that all? Just one assistant cook?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, there were two of them. Well, you know, their stove was only so big, you know, I think that. There were three ovens in that one. I guess they would... Well, they're laying stoves there, fine with wood. I don't know, it must have been eight, nine feet long, I think. Maybe it's ten foot down there, though. There were three ovens, two fireplaces, and the top was big enough because we had the hot cake and girdles, you know, that they could put on. And to get a room for all of that, you know, you take it when they made hot cake for probably around 250, was even for breakfast. And yesterday I was probably sleeping, working nights, you know, and they were probably in bed. To increase out, so forth, come in, they laid their head in the night. There was that baker, you know, he served dinner at midnight. He worked nights, he didn't work in the daytime, there was not room for the room, the stove wasn't big enough. Kitchen wasn't big enough, you know, so the baking was always done at night. After he got cleaned up in the evening and set the table set to novoland, he went to work. And he was there all night. He made, oh, he must have made around, anywhere from 17 and 19 loaves of bread every day, every night. Three, four dozen pie and that many cakes and I don't know how much doughnuts he made, hundreds of them. And cookies and so forth, you know, he worked all night. At the same time, you know, he fed them the night crew that was on, probably 20 or more that he had feeding in the middle of the night. They come in for dinner.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, quite a few.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And all night and all day, you know, everybody come in for lunch, they had a cup of coffee, doughnut, piece of pie. One go to another one come in, sometimes three or four of them. They never, cook never say anything, they go and help themselves, help themselves. They know where it was. In between meals.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I did. I think I had more meals between meals than I did at the table.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I go in the kitchen and have something and then I probably wouldn't see them for lunch. Long after supper was over, I come in and I go and get a little something, whatever I could pick up.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were they starting to use some machinery by that time? You know, chainsaws?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, they didn't come for, hmm, there in the, oh after World War II in the 40s. See, I saw one, we went over one time to Winchester. Up there near Kade Mount.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's somewhere up on top of there where you can go from Lewiston and get over to them where they were logging or you can go up to,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: up to, through Kolosac and go on up the hill there and get up to Krieg Mount. But we, I think we went up there, we went in there on the, to Lewiston.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What do you call that lake up there on top?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When you go to Lewiston and up on there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Wohaw, Wohaw Lake, Wohaw Lake, or something like that. Well, there were logging in there, you see, we went out there and they had a power saw. I forget whether it was, but I think there was a gas one they had. But they had a five foot blade I think, one on each end, cut and timber with. They were doing most of the, they just knocked them down, felled them, you see. And they had, they had cats and they pulled them in three lengths into the landing and they knocked them off. But then shortly after that, you know, they come to those little pallets where you could stay on the landing and knock them off. That's where the saving come in and just one man then. That's when they start to come in fast. But that was later in the 40s.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were they gas powered, the first ones you saw?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, we had the one, we had one, the first one was electric. Used to, we had a little generator on a little cat there, you know, pulled it along. That worked pretty good too. But not so handy as the later one. Now they're really perfect, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I will build a bridge one time. That was the last work I did there. We build a bridge across the North Fork, 350 feet across the river, drew piling and it was 35 feet up in the air. And we cut all the timber in the woods, leveled them off with the power saw. Had the shock line, you know, and you know how shock line is, how they work. To get the straight line, you know, to cut the top off it. I don't think I do. Well, we made it then. We used the power saw, leveled them off, you know. By God, it was just as straight as if you cut it with the band saw in the middle. Put it up and then shocked her down, you know, we set her on the cap on the piling.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That's quite a undertaking, to build that over there. In the middle of it there was a big bent rock, bedrock under the gravel. We used the dozer and shoved the river over this way and banked it up so that it run there and then we worked on the other side and drew pilings. We drilled that 11 foot holes and loosened up that rock and then drew piling in it. And they were using it up until now, I guess it's underwater, if they didn't tear it down. That was up near Lou Bowl's cabin, way up. 350 feet across there we had, I don't know how many pilings we drew. We drew a lot of them. But it stayed doing high water for a truckload.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That road come up to, we crossed the river twice. Crossed over below and then about 10 miles farther up we crossed back again and hit Cedar Creek. That went up there to Busser Roost and way up there in the mountain towards Avery.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember when they took the horses out for the last time?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I see they're coming back now. I see in the paper today, or not today, yesterday or the before, it's an outfit in Montana. They took one million and a half and they're going to lug it with horses.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, that was in 1939 there. No, wait a minute. Yeah, 1939 I think it was. We were out down Mason Meadow when we got the first catch. Well, we had a few before then, but after that they let loose of the horses. So when we moved to 35 we had no horses and I was wishing we had the old horses back. Here the old cats were sitting in the side of the barn, stuck, mired down, couldn't get up the hill. If I had horses there I could have got hoodles of those.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There's a ring I carried for over 50 years.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What does it say? That's Mason, you're a Catholic, aren't you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I thought you said you worked down to the home. Oh, I just went over there. I was over there when Joe and his wife had their anniversary. Joe Holland. I was in the Columbus Hall there.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: A few years ago, not so many, is it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How long ago was that? Five years? Four years? Five years, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I went over there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I met quite a few. That's where I met, you saw again Jack Downey. He was gray headed. Gray as a white now. He's pretty white. I think I'm older than he is. I'm sure I am by seven years. Oh yes, I went down to Rosa's store yesterday and there was a lady sitting in a pickup there next door.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I came back next to her and when I opened the door and she said, how old are you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, I'm pretty old.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: She said, are you 71?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, yes, I'm 71. I'd seen that. I said, I'll talk to her for quite a while. She said she had to get out. She said she didn't like the town. I had to get out in the woods. I said, well, that's where I was raised. I said, I put in lots and lots of years in the woods.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: She said, do you feel good yet?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said, I feel fine. I feel fine. I said, my legs are wore out, that's all.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She said she got to get out in the woods and get away from town. I don't like it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yes, it is rain, not blue. Oh, look at there, that high -ended.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: See it? That plane?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now we see what it is.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Does he go through that cloud?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, he's under it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh, we got darn it. He's under that cloud too. I can still see it. I can see it, but I can't hear it now.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That's pretty good, isn't it, for an old buck like me? That's many miles away, isn't it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I'd have sat there once in a while, you know, and I'd see a test myself that way and see if I can see them when I lose the sound of them. By God, I can see them a little bit farther if it's clear, if we don't get into a cloud. The sound will die out, you know, and I still can see them. No glasses.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Is there something in that, you know, that tells me now my hair don't turn gray very fast, you know, probably just solid concrete under it, nothing under it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is that right?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That the air keeps going because there was nothing under it. I never heard that. There might be something to it. It's true though, your hair isn't, your hair is, you know, a lot of it's brown. I get gray hair, I had to cut this straight off. I got a little, right here, they get gray or white. Laura's getting white hairs already too, a few. She's got gray hairs. Oh yes, well, you know, some people get gray hair when they're in, before 30 or in 30's.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I guess they change the color, that, I guess it don't hurt nothing.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: It don't hurt anything, does it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I know I can't feel anything bad over it. Well, at least you got a hair. A lot of people ask me, how come that your hair stays so good when I say that's the reason for everything. I say there's nothing under it. Just growing there and I say no reason for it to turn gray, I guess.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Axel, I was wondering about the strike of 36. You talked to us about it just a little bit before, but I was wondering about what caused it and what you remember from it. I don't know what caused it, I could not tell you. There was a strike for higher wages.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And there was nobody in the camp that worked really for hourly wage, only the cook -offs, the section crews, and the train crews. They were the only ones that worked hourly wages. The others had contractors, YIPO, and they made twice what the wages was. And they didn't strike. They were satisfied where they were.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: So what put it on?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And who struck them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, they lumberjacked. They walked out on us, or started to. But they said they were striking over wages. They really wanted that to work for their wages. They were satisfied. They wasn't even in on it. Well, they were in the camp, but then the others... They had, I think, just the educators worked the crew up. The educators would go around from one camp to the other and preach how they didn't get enough, and they should have more, and all of this and that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I never knew what it was. Because it turned out to be there, we didn't have no strike. We gave them that weekly after every 40 year I. When they came back, we never heard the word. They kept working, and long before they were here, the camp was full again. We never stopped. They did on the Clearwater side. They were shut down for a while, but on the potlatch end there, we kept on going.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: You shut down a week at the Fourth of July just to stop a strike?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, well, no. The Fourth of July was coming. When I talked to an oracle there, he was the superintendent, and I said, let's just give them ten days or something like that for the Fourth of July vacation. They go out anyway, so let's give it to them now.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: We did. I told them and the crew there, I called up the camp up above there too, Melchior, my brother and that. I told them the same thing, and I said, I told them here that they want to go out, okay. We'll pay them all off, pay them all off. I said, anybody wants to come back after the Fourth of July, I have the train crew in Boville, and they'll be ready. The day after, two days after the Fourth was over, I said, we'll have to plant the flat garden. I said, bring them all back to camp again. Take them home. We'll give them a ride out too. I did. I said, we're up there about noon, if you have it ready, you let us know when you have them, checks ready for them, let us know, and I'll send the same train crew up. Keep feeding them. It's not never happened until the end. Breakfast the next morning, dinner the same as usual. I put them all on the train and back out here, I got them all back for seven. The whole crew come back.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: By when?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: By seven. Seven men, all that stayed out. I got the whole crew back there after the ten days was over. I don't know, and I said, you give a jack ten dollars and he's hard to talk to. I said, let him stay in town and blow it all in. I said, by God, we can all talk to him. I said, he'll be back. He'll be back. He did it too.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I got a little pamphlet every day, however it come in there, I don't know, that the strikers were putting out. I think they were, I don't know if it come from Spokane or, I don't know where it come from, where they printed it, but I got one every day.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They always say see to it that I got one and they said, excellent Finks. They call them all Finks. Towards the end there, you know, they send out 32 carloads every day from them two camps. They said, I won't be long now. He's got nothing on the college kids and they'll be gone. They'll be out of there. By God, we never showed up. There was a few. We had a lot of college kids there. Then there was others drifting in. I had a crew all the time.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did some of the other camps around there have more trouble?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, on the Clearwater side, they did have. We only had the two camps and they were both together only two miles apart. They were on the railroad and we had them two camps there and they kept going.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: The pamphlets said Axle right in the pamphlet that you got? He talked about you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Axle and his Finks.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They were little that evening. They thought, now he'll come in. He was the superintendent and the sheriff will come in. That's why they thought I had. I knew that they were coming and I didn't. I didn't know that the sheriff was coming. I don't think Naugle did either.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: But he must have had a sheriff of Summerfield and Moody and what's the other? Jordan?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Moody and Jordan and Summerfield. They were all there and they popped up there. I didn't know. But the Latinam people blamed me. They thought that I had called them in and I had not asked anybody. I was innocent on that one. But they started to have a fight there and then every time they were up on there were three loaded gravel carts there. They were gondolas. They were standing on top of that. So Summerfield all around and he said, no fighting. He said, be quiet. And this perch, he says, or I'll throw you. He had them smoke bombs with them and he said, I'll throw some of this into you. By God they're quieting now. It's all over.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But they were going to set out. They wanted my crew to go with them up and watch what they did. So the boat crews come back after all the talking they had done.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did the sheriffs make arrests while they were out?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But we had, we had, because they had their pickets on every road leading in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And Moody, he come out for one. He stayed there in a tent up above the camp leading out, the road leading to Southwick. Him and another friend, I guess they must have been there. Well, all the till late in the fall. Two months or more that he set up there. We carried shock up to him every day from the camp. And then we had another one on the rail head down to, to around Meadow. That was the only two roads leading in there, you see, on the railroad. But they never bothered us any.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Who, the pickets or the police?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The pickets. They never bothered any. They never come in. I used to go through them, talk to them, ever so often. I knew all of them there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: On the Clearwater they had little trouble. I guess they had a few fights there, you know.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But they, they camp was shut down there I guess for a couple of months or more.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But there was nothing settled there really. Next winter in the fall there I think was shut down.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It was shut down in November then, the next fall. There was nothing ever settled.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Did you say that there were guys that quit that wanted to come back and you wouldn't let them?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I wasn't thinking of any names, but I was just thinking of the, of guys that changed their minds about that strike. No, I think they were, most of them was back.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, I think they all come back, most of them. Because they had just, they had just blew over. There was no settlement in that strike at all. They just had to walk out and after they come back in they were all satisfied. Same thing, we didn't change anything in the camp that I know of.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know anything about the bullpens and that first strike?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, I was in France.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: That was, so that was after the strike?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I was gone then. I was here, I was working there when the, when the struck. But I heard about it, but no, I didn't know anything about it because I was out of the country.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I had a nice good chum there in France and the Wobblies killed him. He was from Centralia, Washington, Deal Hubbard. He got in a fight with somebody and he took after him. I couldn't, that's just what I read in the paper. But it was kind of a parac or something that I would, I went through Centralia a few times because I couldn't, couldn't tell you anything about it. But anyway, he took after one there and I'll be there and he can get down there in the woods and brush and only shot him. The last time you heard of the, pretty much of the Wobblies, that 36 strike, was that the... Oh, we had a little strike up there in, 48 I think it was. We were shot down for, oh, for a couple of months. But that, that ended about the same way. That's the last I guess I ever heard the Wobblies. So he got the CIO come in and the AF of L took over. I guess that ended. That was the last time, real Wobblies talk we ever heard of.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Some way that just, that big, they wanted to have one big union, what they said. I guess that ended it. It didn't pan out for some reason.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Nobody knows what the deal was after, I couldn't. But they done, they done one good thing that we all got to give up. Even crazy for, they took out the blankets. Part of the, the companies had to buy blankets and clean up the cars and make more room, make more, better living in the camp. That, that much, the one that was during the World War I.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I know when I left for France and left the camp there, I think we had 16 bunks in each car. When I come back they were down to eat half of that. And the company was finished in all the beddings and clean sheets and pedal slips. Oh that made quite a difference in the camp. You bet you. That was needed, it was necessary. I don't think the company felt bad over the other countries. And they got to eat our day. That come in at that time when we were from daylight to dark before, you know. When I left them we come back to eat ours. I just got started when I, and here the crew come in, they were, eat ours up at four o 'clock and well you know the sun was up, too high for me. When I left you know we had a couple good hours work after that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So dear Bob, you done a lot of good.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When I come back they had a union there, they call it the four L's.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That kind of that wore out. They tried you know to hold anything to hold the crew.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And it didn't work out, that died out and then after that they come back.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I can't tell you, I don't know what the hell they call that union, there was a con about.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But it didn't seem to work out till they got the F of L in. Then it kind of settled down again quite a bit, that was pretty good. I never belonged to a union in my life, in any of them.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: If you were in the supervisory and you couldn't, they wouldn't let you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: When men complain to you about you know something that was bothering them at the camp.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What did they usually complain about?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh I don't know. If I heard anything I tried to correct it. Oh I don't know what did. Had nothing in particular to it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: If a guy couldn't work with someone else would you transfer?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, I tried to change him, change him around.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: There was one day, one morning come in, that was Charlie Yellaburg, you talked about him the other day.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: He come in one morning and says I'm bunching it, I'm going down.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I said what's wrong Charlie?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No he said nothing, I just quit.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And I said I don't think so, I said Charlie you want a different swamper don't you?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I said you want a different man to work with you.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Is that it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, no I'm quitting. Well I said that. There was a fellow working with him, his name was Bill Zimmerman. They were, oh they, I don't know if we went to school together but the one, one was raised out on Cedar Creek and the other one was Charlie Yellaburg was raised at Park, only a few miles apart where they flipped. So I said I'll take Bill away from you, I said I'll be, I said so and so with you, go on out. He took the team and he went back to work. No more said about it. Well that, you know, that was just, they had, they got so much a thousand, they wasn't working by the hourly rate, they were working by so much a thousand, the more logs they get in the more money they made.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And more logs you could get, you know, the cheaper you got them. That didn't cut too much difference on the price. If you give you lots of logs, you know, you could pay them more and you still got cheap logs.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The idea was the more logs you get in the camp, they cut down on overhead and that cut down, you know, everything was based on the logs you put in. The cost of the logs was, that take care of, that was the base of all the logging in the camps and everywhere. More logs you could get, you know, you could spread it out and they cut down all over.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I didn't produce any logs, I was just walking down there and, well you know, the more logs they get, you know, they cut down on paying my salaries, whatever I got. Cut down on everybody and I was like the blacksmith, they didn't produce anything. Cocoa screw didn't produce anything. They didn't pull in any logs. So you had to get, the base was to get more logs so you could get the cheaper logs you have. The logs didn't come in, you know, everything was run up.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: The last log is the one to get. If they were yippo, you know, if they tried to get as many logs as they possibly could, you know, well then we got cheap logs. And if you were going the other way and trying to just put the day in, you know, you didn't get any saw logs. That's what the foreman's job is to try to see, to get all the saw logs he possibly can get out of the woods.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Were your camps largely yippo, you know, the men, or would they be?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, towards the later years they were mostly all yippos.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know, for some reason, you know, when it started after World War I, we used to let them contract and pay them day wages as long as they were working. And we set a price on them, on the logs to them. But we didn't, and at the end, at the end of the job when they were finished, then you called us a bonus, you see. Everybody was paid just as much as they were paid the daily wages. And then at the end of the job, when they were done, then they divided up the bonus according to days they put in on the whole job. A man that quit in the meantime, he didn't get done. That was the bonus, that was left for the other federal to divide up. So we had one job there in particular that I remember, I think that lasted 18 months. And I believe there was something around $30 ,000 worth of bonus there when they ended up. In them days, you know, that was a hell of a lot of money. That was in the 20s. And that was a lot of money.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How big a crew would work together on a job?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: They had a highline donkey, they were 21 I think. I think there were three gangs of sawyers at 6.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And let's see, a fireman, and there was two, there'd be eight. A top loader would be nine.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Let's see how they had to figure it out.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: See the engineer, the engineer and the fireman,

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: and the top loader, two hookers, and one of the laddies.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And then they had four to six saw gangs in the woods.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And a whistle punk, and a hook tender, and two hookers at it, there'd be four more. Yeah, there were around 20 men anyway in that crew.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Huh?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, two in the saw gang. One on each end of the crosscut saw. Yeah, that's one of them.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: What did the hook tender do?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Well, he is the one that, he's the bossy man on the job, you see, on that particular job. He's the one that, you see, that the rigging set up and kept running.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: It's the two, the one that run the machine and the hook tender, that's the two head men on the crew, you see.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, they were good. They were Adrian Pelton, he run the donkey, and Homer was hook tender, the one that's in pot light. And the brother, Lotus, he was the whistle punk, no, he was the hoister. I believe the old man was the whistle punk. He blew the whistle out in the woods. Old man, U .S. Pelton, the old man. And there was one more, by God, if I remember right, there was still another Pelton in there.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And Adrian was married, and she run the cook house, they run their own cook house, and they were really a Jepu. So we call that place there the Jepuville, or the Peltonville, Peltonville, what you call it. Most, there was, well, the old family there, you know, the old Pelton family, they stayed there in a tent and stayed pretty close to the yard. There was a few more in there, but they were in the camp at the cook house.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Now that was a big donkey, they went out a mile. They had a mile cable tight line out to get the sole off.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: That was the last donkey they stripped, they sent to the scrap pile. They call that a spotlight special. I guess that was the only one ever made. I think that had five drums on it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Would you tell us a story of the time that you got hurt in the snow plow?

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I think I did, didn't I?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I don't know. Well, there ain't much to tell about it. We went over the side of the bridge, and I went down in the river with two more in that went to me.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I still have it. I can't raise that arm, only that far, that's it. That's as far as I can get that.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And that was hanging there for two years. But anyway, I went to a lot of doctors. And all of them told me the same story, if you ever want to get any good of it, you got to do it yourself. We can't help you.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: I went in there to see a bone specialist, I forget his name now, he was in the medical building. And I drove from Boville down, and I got in pretty late. I guess I was late in the afternoon. But I had no appointment with him, I just drove over to see him on my own hook.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: The girl asked me, she said, have you got an appointment?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, no, no appointment.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: And she said, do we have to have an appointment?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And I said, is Dr.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: So -and -so in there?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Oh yes, yes he is. I said, I come 150 miles to see him. And I said, I'd like to see him. But after hours.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I said, that's too bad, I come all the way and I said, you wouldn't even let me see him?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She said, no, it's after hours, he's too, the office is closed.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I said, well, is the doctor in there now?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: And she said yes.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: I said, this is the only way out, he come out this way?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: She said yes, and I said, you tell him. Anyway, I said, I'm going to see him. I'm going to stay right here. She left and little Betsy walked in there and the two of them come out and they were both left. And I, yes, she said, here I am. I said, I am here too. I said, I come a long ways to see you. She said, I don't know if you can do me any good or not, but I said, I thought I'd see you anyway, even if you do anything or not. She said, come on in.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So he examined me and took a picture and so forth. He said, no, I can't do nothing for you. So while I got out of it, he got $20 and I left.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But anyway, I got the satisfaction of seeing him. It's all men.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, Gibson told me that in Potlatte. And then I saw Armstrong there in Moscow. He told me the same thing. I went to Jacobson down in Lewiston. He told me the same thing. I went to Maury up to Wallace. He told me the same thing. All of them told me exactly the same thing. He said, and nobody can do anything for you. He said, you got to do it. So I did, I got a pretty good grip on it. I could get that back. But I think I was sure, you know, they thought I'd never come out of it. They thought I was gone. I think Gibson was sure that I didn't make it. So they never, they never, if they put me in the cast, you don't put that up, I think, and work them muscles up a little bit around. I believe they could have done something. Maybe, that's my way. That could have helped me to get that back. But they just hung it up like this, you know. And from there on, it was up to me. I only stayed in the hospital three weeks.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how the accident happened? I mean, what caused it?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, we were out of here. We were out of here, you see. The front, the front dropped down on the rail on the middle of the bridge. And it cut somewhere, you see, that I didn't know, but I'd seen it. See, I tried to blow the whistle and it had no air. They didn't blow. Some way, you see, something went wrong with the air. Oh, I know what to do with it. It was all done.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: No, that could have been the engineer's fault. And mine too, yours as well. I had the gates up there and I didn't watch it.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: But I think that was it.

speaker: Laura or Sam Schrager
words: How did they get you? They just got you down the podlatch from there right away?

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: Yeah, they come and help me. The luck was, you see, that the engineer had a piece of mine enough to horse that lever over and get the locomotive in reverse motion. So when the snowplow uncoupled, you see, when it broke over, it uncoupled from the locomotive. And the snowplow and us, we went down in the river and the engineer backed down the road again, backed the bicycle.

speaker: Axel Anderson
words: So they come and helped us. Oh, I remember part of the day when I walked through here, they were telling me there's no power supply and I said put it out. I used to let it burn up. I remember that. Then I remember when I got down to the locomotive, they helped me up and I don't know how in the heck I got up in there. I couldn't tell you how I got up in that, on the locomotive up in the cabin there. I don't know. In the cab I don't know. But I was sitting on the sandbox there at I come to again.
